actions.thought: I am on the button with Ad 8d, a strong suited Ace that is well within the standard opening range for a 3-handed game. Opening to 3x the big blind is a standard play to exert pressure and build a pot while having positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

I am holding King-Three offsuit in the Small Blind position, which is a very weak hand that lacks playability, especially when facing a raise from the button. Calling or re-raising with such a marginal holding would force me to play out of position against a range that likely dominates mine, leading to a negative expected value over the long run. Folding is the most disciplined and professional choice to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with K6 offsuit, a weak holding. The Button has raised to 3x the big blind. Playing a weak, uncoordinated hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser is a losing proposition in the long run. Folding is the best decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In a 3-handed game, the button is a highly advantageous position. Although Queen-Eight offsuit is a marginal holding, it is strong enough to open from the button to attempt to steal the blinds. Raising to 250 puts pressure on the small and big blinds and allows me to play in position.

Holding 82s in the Small Blind facing a Button raise is an easy fold. The hand is extremely weak, offers very little playability post-flop, and being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor makes it completely unprofitable to continue.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. While I am getting decent pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 400), K4o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Defending with such a marginal hand against a button raise often leads to difficult post-flop spots where I am dominated or forced to fold. At this early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities rather than bleed chips in a high-variance, out-of-position spot.

With Ace-Deuce suited on the Button in a 3-handed game, we have a very reasonable hand to open-raise. We can make the nut flush and have high card value with the Ace. Raising to 2.5x the big blind (250) applies pressure to the blinds and allows us to play in position if called.

I have a pocket pair of fives in the Small Blind. Pro raised to 250 from the Button. 55 is a strong enough hand to see a flop with, primarily for set mining. Calling 200 more into a pot of 400 gives me good pot odds and excellent implied odds if I hit a set on the flop. A 3-bet is an option, but calling allows me to keep the pot smaller and play post-flop.

I hold Jack-3 offsuit, which is a weak hand with poor connectivity and low equity. Although the pot odds of 4:1 (calling 150 to win 600) are tempting, playing a weak hand out of position against two opponents is negative expected value. It is best to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

I called pre-flop with pocket fives looking for a set. The flop of Ad-9h-3h contains an Ace, which is a card that hits the pre-flop raiser's (Pro) range very well. Being out of position, I will check to the aggressor to see how they play this board. I have a small pair, but it is likely beat by any Ace or a nine, so I am prepared to fold if there is significant pressure.

I flopped top pair but have the weakest possible kicker. The board is quite wet with a flush draw present. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot gets value from weaker pairs like a 9, pocket pairs, and flush draws, while keeping the pot manageable in case my opponent has a better Ace.

The flop contains an Ace, which is a card very much in the pre-flop aggressor's (Pro) range. My pocket fives are now a weak pair, likely trailing any Ace or Nine. Pro has made a standard continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot. While the price to call is relatively cheap, I am out of position and have very few ways to improve my hand. Continuing with pocket fives on this board is likely to lead to further losses on later streets, so I will fold now and wait for a better spot.

In a 3-handed game, holding Q2s on the button is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. Since I have position and the hand has some post-flop playability (being suited), I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to try and take the pot now or play a pot in position.

Holding Ten-Four suited in the small blind against a button open is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Defending with such a weak hand would lead to difficult post-flop decisions and poor equity realization. Folding is the most disciplined and high-value play here to preserve my stack.
82o is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, there is absolutely no reason to call or raise. Folding is the only sensible and profitable choice here.

Ten-six suited is a weak hand, even on the button. It lacks the strength to call or raise profitably against two opponents. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more premium hand to engage with, as my position here does not justify playing such a marginal holding.

With K5o in the small blind, the hand is very weak and difficult to play out of position post-flop. While attempting a steal is an option, it is generally better to fold these marginal offsuit holdings and preserve our stack for better spots.
JTo is a very reasonable hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. I have position and a hand with good playability, so I will raise to 2.5x to steal the blinds or play a pot in position.

Holding Queen-Deuce offsuit in the small blind is a very weak starting hand. Pro has raised from the button, which often indicates a wide range, but Q2o does not play well post-flop out of position and has poor equity against even a wide opening range. Folding is the most prudent action to conserve chips for more favorable situations.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit against a button open from Pro. The pot odds are 250 to call 150, which is approximately 2.33:1. Ace-Five offsuit is a defendable hand in this position against a button steal range. I have sufficient stack depth and getting the right price to see a flop and evaluate. A 3-bet is possible, but given the early stage of the tournament, calling is a solid, standard play to realize equity.

I am out of position in the big blind with Ace-high on a board of Td-6h-8s. Since the opponent raised pre-flop, they are likely to continue with a continuation bet on this board. Checking allows me to evaluate the size of their bet and my opponent's range. Betting here would be a mistake as it essentially turns my hand into a bluff against a range that likely hit this flop better than my Ace-high.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a T-6-8 rainbow board. The board is somewhat connected, so there are straight draws (like 97, J9) and worse pairs (like an 8 or a 6, or worse tens) that can call a bet. Flashlight checked to me, so I will make a continuation bet to extract value and protect my hand from overcards and draws.

I am out of position with Ace-high on a board of Td-6h-8s. The opponent has continued with a bet, representing strength or a piece of the board such as a pair of tens or eights. My hand holds no pair, no flush draw, and minimal straight equity, making it a weak bluff-catcher that is likely behind the opponent's range. Given the tournament context and stack depth, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid bleeding chips in a marginal spot without clear continuation equity.

J3s is a weak hand to open from the button, especially with an active player like Pro in the Big Blind. While I could attempt a steal, I'll maintain a tighter range this early in the tournament and fold.

I am in the small blind with 3-5 offsuit, a very weak hand. Since the button folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Calling would require putting in an additional 50 chips with a hand that has very low equity and poor playability. It is mathematically and strategically superior to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

8c4c is a weak suited connector with very low equity and poor playability. Calling the 100 big blind from the button with this hand is a negative expected value play, as it does not perform well against the range of the small blind and big blind. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal action.

With Jack-Three suited in the small blind, my hand is too weak to profitably play out of position against the big blind. Even though the button folded and I only have to call 50, playing marginal hands out of position often leads to difficult, negative expected-value situations. Folding the small blind and preserving the stack for a better spot is the most disciplined choice.

I am dealt 7d 5c on the button. This is a very weak, unsuited two-gapper with minimal equity and poor playability post-flop. Even in a 3-handed game where opening ranges are wider, this hand falls into the bottom tier of starting hands. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold and wait for a better spot to attack the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit (J3o). The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. J3o is a very weak hand, and while it is tempting to complete the blind for only 50 more chips or try a steal raise, this hand plays poorly post-flop and out of position. I will choose the disciplined route and fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours. In a 3-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to try and take down the blinds or play a pot in position. A standard 2.5x opening raise to 250 is appropriate here to exert pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I face resistance.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 9d, which is a weak hand with poor playability. Calling out of position against a button raise would be negative expected value and complicates the hand needlessly. Folding is the most disciplined approach to preserve my stack early in this 150-hand tournament.
75o is a weak offsuit one-gapper. Facing a raise from the Button, it is an easy fold from the Big Blind as it lacks both playability and equity.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to open in a three-handed game. I want to apply pressure to the small and big blinds to take down the blinds and ante immediately. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) is an appropriate size to accomplish this without overcommitting if I face a reraise.

Facing a button open, pocket twos are very marginal in the small blind. We are out of position, and the big blind still acts behind us, presenting a squeeze risk. Our hand almost strictly relies on hitting a set (which happens about 1 in 8 times) to win a large pot. Being out of position reduces our implied odds because it is harder to build the pot when we do hit, and harder to win the pot when we miss. Folding is the safest and most standard choice.

Q7o is a weak hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.6:1, playing this hand out of position against a range that likely includes better Queens and high cards is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack.
I am on the Button with a very weak hand (2s 4h). There is no reason to get involved with this hand. Folding is the best and only viable option.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has folded. While it's only 50 more to call, this hand plays poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Raising is also not ideal here as 9-4o has little equity if called. I will fold to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ad 2h. In a 3-handed game, any Ace is typically strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot immediately. A raise to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a standard opening size in this position.

I am in the small blind holding Queen-Three offsuit. This is a very weak hand, especially when playing out of position against a button raise. Defending with this hand is generally negative expected value, so the standard and correct play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

2d 3d is a very weak suited connector. Although we are getting decent pot odds (150 to win 400), we will be playing out of position against the pre-flop raiser. This hand rarely makes strong, dominating hands and is prone to reverse implied odds, especially when hitting flushes. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

Queen-Three offsuit is a bottom-tier starting hand. Even in a shorthanded three-handed game, this hand lacks the strength and connectivity required to justify an open-raise or a call from the button. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision.
With T7o in the small blind against the big blind, the hand is too weak and unsuited to play out of position. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.
J7s is a marginal hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. Without strong reads that the blinds over-fold, it's safer to just fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 2h. The Button has folded. While it's only 50 more to call, 9-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Playing it out of position against the Big Blind is unlikely to be profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a three-handed game. T3s is a marginal hand, but in this format, it is standard to open a wide range of hands from the button to put pressure on the blinds. My previous button raises have successfully taken down the blinds without a fight (Hands 1, 4, 10, 13). I will continue this aggressive approach by raising to 250, looking to win the pot immediately or play the hand with positional advantage.

Ten-Nine offsuit is a marginal holding to play out of position against a button raise. Defending from the small blind with this hand against a standard open is typically negative expected value because the hand is easily dominated and playing post-flop without position is difficult. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I have a positional or range advantage.

KTo is a marginal offsuit broadway hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a button raise, despite the attractive pot odds against a likely wide range, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make folding a disciplined and lower-variance choice.

64 offsuit is a marginal hand with very little showdown value or potential to connect strongly with the board. In a 3-handed game, even on the button, there is no incentive to play such a weak holding. It is optimal to fold and wait for a more premium hand to engage in the action, preserving my stack for better spots.

34o is an extremely weak, disconnected hand. Even though I only have to call 50 in the small blind, playing out of position against the big blind with trash is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
J7o is a very weak starting hand, not suitable for an open even from the Button. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 7c. The Button (Pro) has folded. This is a Blind vs. Blind situation. While I could attempt to steal the Big Blind with a raise, 5s 7c is a very weak hand to play out of position if called. Having successfully stolen several blinds recently, I don't want to over-leverage my table image with such a poor starting hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 6c7c, which is a strong speculative hand in a 3-handed game. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds, or play a pots-position advantage post-flop. A standard 2.5x raise is effective.

I am in the Small Blind with J3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Playing this out of position against a button open is mathematically unfavorable and will likely lead to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the standard and most profitable play given the strength of my cards.
4d9h is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Against a button open-raise, folding from the big blind is the standard and correct play to conserve chips.

63 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with extremely limited potential for post-flop equity. Even in a three-handed game, playing this hand from the button is not profitable. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

With the Button folding, I have a decent suited gapper (Q8s) in the Small Blind. This is a profitable spot to attempt a steal against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 applies maximum pressure to take down the pot uncontested pre-flop while giving me a playable hand if called.

King-Jack of clubs is a very strong hand to defend the Big Blind with, especially in a heads-up situation against the Small Blind. Given that I have position for the remainder of the hand and KJs plays excellently post-flop, calling is a standard and effective play to keep the opponent's wider range in the pot while controlling the size of the pot.

As the pre-flop aggressor on a dry, disconnected flop (T-2-6), my range has a significant advantage, containing all the overpairs and strong tens. My hand, Qc8c, has a backdoor flush draw and overcards to the middle and bottom pair. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot is typically enough to fold out the BB's completely missed hands and broadway overcards that failed to connect, allowing me to take down the pot without much resistance.

On a dry flop of Tc-2d-6h, the opponent has led with a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot. I hold King-Jack of clubs, giving me two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. Given the pot odds of 4:1 and my positional advantage, calling is the most efficient play. It allows me to see a turn card and potentially improve or take the pot away if the opponent checks on later streets, without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

The Ace of spades is a perfect scare card on the turn, heavily favoring my perceived pre-flop raising range and flop continuation betting range. Flash's flop calling range is mostly marginal pairs like weak tens, middle pairs (77-99), or floats that missed. Firing a second barrel here applies immense pressure on these hands, likely forcing folds from everything except an Ace or a set. Since my hand has zero showdown value, turning it into a bluff is the most profitable play.

The Turn card is the Ace of Spades, which is a very strong card for the Small Blind's opening range. Pro has followed up their flop continuation bet with a half-pot bet on the turn. My hand, King-Jack high, has no showdown value and no significant draws on this board. Continuing would require a pure bluff on the river or hitting a marginal pair that still loses to any Ace. Given the board texture and the opponent's betting line, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.
QTo is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the Button in a 3-handed game. I'll raise to 2.5x to try and steal the blinds or play in position with a decent holding.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit (As 4c). The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. While Ace-Four is a playable hand in many situations, out of position it is relatively weak against a standard button opening range, especially one that has shown aggression in previous hands. However, folding to every raise allows opponents to exploit my blinds. In a 3-handed game, A4o is often ahead of a wide button range. Calling is an option to see a flop, but playing out of position without the initiative is difficult. Folding is conservative but safe to preserve my stack of 98.5bb. Given the tournament context and the need to protect my stack while looking for better spots, I will fold this time and wait for a stronger holding or a better positional advantage.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Eight offsuit. Pro has raised from the button, and the pot odds are roughly 3.6-to-1, making this a clear defend situation. While Q8o is not a premium holding, it has sufficient equity to call against a button opening range. Folding would be too conservative given the current pot size and my position.

My hand, Queen-eight offsuit, has completely missed the flop of nine-three-ten. With no pair, no draw, and being out of position, I have zero incentive to lead the betting. Checking is the only logical play as I wait to see if my opponent bets or checks behind. I will look to fold if a significant bet is placed.

I flopped top pair with a strong Queen kicker on a wet board containing a flush draw and several straight draw possibilities. Checking back would give free cards to my opponent's drawing hands. Betting around half-pot charges those draws and extracts value from worse made hands, such as weaker Tens or Nines.

The board of 9s-3c-Ts completely missed my Queen-Eight offsuit. With no pair and no significant draw, I am essentially drawing dead against the majority of the opponent's range. The opponent's continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot indicates strength or a standard continuation strategy, and calling here would be throwing away chips with no clear path to equity. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9-7 offsuit. This is a marginal hand that is generally outside of a standard opening range even in a 3-handed game. While I could attempt a steal, the table has shown some resistance, and I want to maintain a tighter image to ensure my future bluffs and value bets are respected. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with Jack-Nine offsuit, which is a playable hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Since the button has folded, I am only facing the big blind. Rather than raising and bloating the pot with a mediocre hand, I will call the additional 50 chips to complete the blind and see a flop.

Flashlight limped from the Small Blind, which often indicates weakness or a speculative hand. With pocket sixes and positional advantage, raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot right now, or build a pot in position if called.

I previously intended to complete the blind, but Pro raised to 300. Jack-Nine offsuit is a weak hand to defend against a 3x raise, especially out of position. Calling 200 more chips to see a flop with a dominated hand is negative expected value, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

4h 7h is a very weak suited connector, and while the button is a position with flexibility, this hand has poor playability and low equity. Given the stack depths and the goal of maximizing long-term profitability, folding is the most disciplined decision to avoid unnecessary risk with a marginal hand.

J8s is a solid starting hand heads-up from the small blind. I'll raise to 300 to apply pressure on the big blind, take the initiative, and likely win the pot uncontested. If called, I have a suited connector that plays reasonably well post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Td 3c. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Although I will have position post-flop, T3o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated and does not play well in raised pots. I have been folding my weaker holdings consistently and will continue to do so here to preserve my stack.

I have a premium hand, AQo, on the Button. This is a clear open-raise to build the pot and assert initiative pre-flop. A raise size of 250 chips (2.5x BB) is standard and consistent with my previous raises.

In this 3-handed game, the Button (Pro) has been very active, raising frequently. I am in the Small Blind with King-Trey suited (Kd 3d). While this hand has some equity and flush potential, playing it out of position against an aggressive player like Pro is difficult. Calling 200 more to play a pot out of position with a marginal hand is often a losing play in the long run. I could consider a 3-bet as a bluff given Pro's wide opening range, but K3s is a bit weak for that at this stack depth. I will fold and look for a better opportunity to take initiative or defend with a stronger hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h-6h. The pot is 400 and I need to call 150. Pro is acting aggressively from the button. Given the 3.6-to-1 pot odds, defending with suited connectors is mathematically acceptable even though the cards are weak. I will see a flop and re-evaluate post-flop.

I am in the big blind with 4h-6h. The flop is 5d-2s-Th. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 3), but otherwise, I have no made hand. Betting here would be a bluff without much equity to back it up. The standard play is to check and re-evaluate if the opponent bets.

I have two strong overcards on a relatively dry and unconnected board (5-2-T rainbow). As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet here will likely win the pot uncontested against many of Flashlight's defending hands that missed the flop. I'll size my bet to 300 chips to put pressure on weak pairs and random floats while remaining consistent with my previous post-flop bet sizing.

I am in the big blind with 4h-6h. The flop is 5d-2s-Th. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 3), but no made hand. The pot is 850 and it costs 300 to call, which gives me pot odds of roughly 26%. Since I only have about 8.5% equity to hit my straight on the turn, this is not a profitable call. I will fold and conserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While 3-handed play allows for a wider opening range from the button, J4o is extremely weak and unlikely to play well post-flop, especially against Pro in the big blind who has been playing aggressively. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit after the Button folded. This is a strong hand heads-up, and I should look to take the initiative against the Big Blind rather than just limping and playing out of position. A raise of 250 is a standard sizing to steal the blinds or build a pot with a superior hand.
My hand, Ten-Three offsuit, is extremely weak and completely unplayable against a raise, even in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Folding is the best choice to conserve chips.

Jack-four offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor equity. Even with the positional advantage of the button, it is not profitable to play this hand in a three-handed game. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent decision to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce suited. While it's a suited hand with a high card, it is very weak and disconnected. If I raise to steal and get called by the Big Blind, I will be out of position post-flop with a hand that struggles to make strong top pairs or draws. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

Being on the button in a 3-handed game, K4s is a decent hand to open with for a steal. My standard raise to 2.5x (250) has been effective at picking up the blinds, and the suited king gives me some equity and flush draw potential if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-8 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. This hand is too weak to call out of position, and 3-betting it would be too loose against an aggressive opponent. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

The button (Pro) is opening a wide range of hands, and I am getting 2.6:1 pot odds to call from the big blind. While Q-6o is a marginal hand, it is standard to defend the big blind against a button open when getting these odds to prevent the opponent from stealing the blinds too frequently.

I am in the big blind out of position on a wet board (3h 6s 7h) that hits the button's (Pro) range quite well. My hand, middle pair with a Queen kicker, is a marginal bluff catcher. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot controlled and evaluate the opponent's continuation bet, as betting into the pre-flop aggressor on this texture is usually inefficient.

My hand completely missed this flop, offering only King-high and no strong draws. However, Flashlight has consistently shown a tendency to play 'fit-or-fold' post-flop, folding to my continuation bets of 300 chips on previous hands (Hands 6, 21, and 24). Applying pressure here with a c-bet of 300 is highly profitable and will likely force a fold from any unpaired hands or weak holdings.

I have middle pair with a Queen kicker on a fairly connected board. The opponent, Pro, is playing aggressively, and this is a standard spot to defend my range. With a pair of sixes, folding to a 300 bet into an 850 pot would be too exploitable and weak. I will call to see the turn, as my hand has decent equity against his continuation betting range, which likely includes overcards or air.

I am holding a pair of sixes on a board that is becoming increasingly coordinated with the addition of the Jack on the turn. My pair is weak, and Pro has demonstrated aggression by betting on the flop. Checking is the optimal strategy here to control the pot size and act as a bluff catcher. Betting into the aggressor with a marginal pair is unlikely to be profitable and risks inflating the pot when I am likely behind.

Flashlight called my flop continuation bet, indicating he connected with the wet 3h-6s-7h board (likely a pair, straight draw, or flush draw). The turn Jc doesn't change much for his range. Since my King-high has virtually no equity and no draws, double-barreling here is too risky against an opponent who finally decided to defend post-flop. I will check behind to control the pot size and take a free card, preparing to fold to most river aggression.

I hold a pair of sixes on a paired board (3-3). The opponent (Pro) checked the turn, signaling potential weakness or a missed draw. My pair of sixes has some showdown value against missed hearts (like Ah-Kh or similar) and lower pocket pairs or air. Betting here would effectively turn my hand into a bluff, which is unnecessary as I am likely behind any Jack or pocket pair higher than sixes. Checking is the optimal line to realize my showdown value and minimize potential losses if the opponent is trapping or has a better hand.

Flashlight checking the river after the turn checked through suggests he likely has a missed draw (like hearts or a straight draw) or a weak one-pair hand. My King-high might occasionally be good against busted draws, but if he holds a weak pair or Ace-high, a bluff is unlikely to succeed since my turn check indicated weakness. Attempting to bluff a calling station who connected with the flop is not a high-percentage play here. Taking the free showdown is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 83s. While it is suited, it is a very weak hand even for 3-handed play. Pro (Big Blind) has been playing actively and I have a decent stack. There is no need to risk chips on a marginal steal with a hand that has poor playability if called. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With 7c 8c in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. 7c 8c is a strong enough hand to play in a heads-up situation, and raising allows me to seize the initiative pre-flop. A raise to 250 is a standard size to put pressure on the Big Blind.

QJo is a strong hand to defend against a Small Blind open. By flatting, I keep the pot manageable, keep his range wide (including hands like worse Qx and Jx), and I will have the positional advantage post-flop.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a somewhat dry board of Jack-Five-Seven. My middle pair of Sevens is likely the best hand currently, but vulnerable to overcards. A continuation bet of 250 (half the pot) allows me to maintain initiative and gain information about the opponent's strength while potentially making hands like Ace-high or gutshot straight draws fold.

I flopped top pair with a strong Queen kicker on a relatively dry board. Flashlight's half-pot continuation bet from the small blind will include many worse hands, such as missed overcards, smaller pairs, and straight draws. Calling is optimal here because it keeps his bluffs in the pot and controls the pot size. Raising might force out weaker hands and isolate me against a tighter range that beats me, like AJ, overpairs, or sets.

The board has become significantly more coordinated with the 9s turn, opening up various straight draws (8-6, T-8, etc.) and potentially hitting the range of an opponent who called on the flop. My pair of 7s has very little showdown value against a calling range that likely includes better pairs, Jacks, or draws. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, so checking is the most strategic play to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river.

The turn brings the 9s, completing some straight draws (like T8 or 86) and introducing a spade flush draw. Flashlight's check indicates weakness, likely a missed bluff, a weaker pair, or a draw that decided to take a free card. With top pair and a strong Queen kicker, I should bet for value and protection to charge draws and extract chips from hands like 9x, TT, or worse Jx. A half-pot bet is appropriate to get calls from these weaker hands while protecting my equity.

The turn 9s makes the board more dangerous, but it also gives me an open-ended straight draw with my 7-8 (needing a 6 or 10 to complete the straight).
